chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper mentions that the source code and simulations are available at a GitHub link (https://github.com/relab/iniva-artifacts), which satisfies the criteria for providing working links to public repositories.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation is conducted by integrating Iniva into an existing implementation of the HotStuff consensus algorithm, using a local cluster with 25 machines and a 10 Gbps TOR switch. This constitutes a physical testbed/real hardware deployment environment.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: The paper provides a clear, explicit section (Section III) detailing the system model, including assumptions about the adversary controlling a fraction 'm' of processes where m <= f = 1/3. It formally defines the problem context around vote omission attacks and incentive compatibility requirements.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper rigorously analyzes trade-offs by presenting simulation results (Figure 2c and Figure 2d) comparing reward loss for victim vs. attacker under different attack scenarios, quantifying the cost of the attack in terms of percentage of block reward lost.

major_weakness: The paper is exceptionally strong across all metrics; the only minor weakness is that the formal definition of the 'Fault Model' (Section III) focuses heavily on Byzantine faults ($m \le f=1/3$) but could benefit from a more explicit enumeration of non-Byzantine failure modes beyond simple process crashes mentioned in Section VIII.
